figures.gloss: The supreme esoteric head of Quimbanda, often syncretized with Lucifer or Baphomet. He is not the Christian epitome of evil, but rather the ultimate spark of rebellion, cosmic fire, and unrestricted freedom.
figures.facet: Unlike the purely chaotic Christian Devil, Maioral is an initiator and emancipator. He represents the raw, amoral power necessary to break structural oppression and achieve material mastery.
figures.provenance: Nicholaj de Mattos Frisvold, 'Pomba Gira and the Quimbanda of Mbumba Nzila' (2011).

figures.gloss: The sovereign of the Quimbanda pantheon in most systematized accounts, ruler of the Seven Kingdoms, who governs the lower zones of the astral plane and directs the working Exus. Individual terreiros (houses) differ sharply on this hierarchy — some do not recognize a distinct Exu Rei separate from Exu Maioral — and the printed sources for the Maioral–Rei structure (dos Ventos, Frisvold, Coppini) are themselves partisan systematizations rather than a codified pan-Quimbanda doctrine.
figures.facet: He commands the 'left path' not as a punisher of sins, but as an absolute monarch of pragmatic forces, demanding respect, pacts, and tribute rather than moral purity.
figures.provenance: Mario dos Ventos, 'Na Gira do Exu' (2006).

figures.gloss: The most famous Pomba Gira, rooted in the historical Maria de Padilla of 14th-century Spain. She commands a vast legion of spirits and is invoked for matters of desperate love, domination, sexual agency, and fierce, unyielding protection.
figures.facet: She alchemizes the European witch-figure and the marginalized Afro-Brazilian woman into an invincible spirit of sovereignty who weaponizes sexuality to subjugate the patriarchal order.
figures.provenance: Kelly E. Hayes, 'Holy Harlots: Femininity, Sexuality, and Black Magic in Brazil' (2011).

figures.gloss: An ancient Exu governing the Kingdom of the Cemetery. He rules over bones, ancestral memory, and necromantic magic, directly consuming the heavy energies of death and decay to work on behalf of the sorcerer.
figures.facet: He acts as the great equalizer. Stripping away the flesh of social status and earthly illusion, Caveira reveals the harsh, bare truth of existence and protects practitioners from lethal curses.
figures.provenance: Nicholaj de Mattos Frisvold, 'Kiumbanda: A Complete Grammar of the Art of Exu' (2006).

figures.gloss: The structural cosmology of Quimbanda, dividing the spiritual and material world into seven domains—such as Crossroads, Cemeteries, Souls, and Forests—each governed by specific legions of Exus holding dominion over those aspects of nature and society.
figures.facet: It maps spiritual power directly onto the mundane geography of the urban and natural environment, asserting that every physical space is a living, accessible altar for sorcery.
figures.provenance: Danilo Coppini, 'Quimbanda: The Cult of the Red and Black Flame' (2019).

figures.gloss: A charismatic 'malandro' or rogue spirit clad in a white suit and red tie. Born in Catimbó and embraced by Quimbanda, he is the master of street-smarts, nightlife, gambling, and navigating impossible odds.
figures.facet: He subverts the legal and social order through cunning and charm rather than raw force, becoming the divine patron of the marginalized hustler surviving in a rigged system.
figures.provenance: Stefania Capone, 'Searching for Africa in Brazil' (2010).

figures.gloss: An iron cauldron or clay pot containing earth, metals, animal blood, and specific sacred objects called 'fundamentos'. This complex magical construction physically anchors an Exu or Pomba Gira to the practitioner's ritual space.
figures.facet: The spirit does not merely visit; it is literally 'seated' and materialized in the physical realm, creating a permanent, localized battery of spiritual power requiring constant feeding and interaction.
figures.provenance: Nicholaj de Mattos Frisvold, 'Pomba Gira and the Quimbanda of Mbumba Nzila' (2011).

figures.gloss: A complex sigil drawn on the ground using pemba (chalk), incorporating arrows, tridents, and crosses. It acts as both a spiritual signature and a cosmological map that summons a specific Exu to the physical plane.
figures.facet: It is not merely symbolic but mechanically functional; drawing the seal temporarily tears a hole in reality, forcing the corresponding spirit to manifest and obey the terms of their pact.
figures.provenance: Danilo Coppini, 'Quimbanda: The Cult of the Red and Black Flame' (2019).

figures.gloss: 'He who locks the streets' — one of the most widely invoked named Exus of the crossroads lines, saluted first in a gira so that the working itself may proceed. He closes roads against enemies and opens them for the client's intent.
figures.facet: He personifies the practical mechanics of Quimbanda cosmology — before any other spirit can act, the road itself (the encruzilhada) must be ritually secured, making him less a distinct personality than the necessary first gatekeeper of every rite.
figures.provenance: Mario dos Ventos, Na Gira do Exu (2006); Nicholaj de Mattos Frisvold, Kiumbanda: A Complete Grammar of the Art of Exu (2006).

figures.gloss: The child-Exus — a distinct class within Quimbanda cosmology, worked in their own line and often given playful, childlike names. They are propitiated with candy and toys but considered dangerous precisely because they act without the self-restraint expected of adult spirits.
figures.facet: Their amorality is unmediated by adult judgment — where Maioral and Exu Rei operate within a recognizable, if inverted, code of pacts and tribute, the Exu Mirim class exposes the tradition's rawest, least domesticated register of spiritual power.
figures.provenance: Nicholaj de Mattos Frisvold, Pomba Gira and the Quimbanda of Mbumba Nzila (2011); Danilo Coppini, Quimbanda: The Cult of the Red and Black Flame (2019).

figures.gloss: The 'Gypsy' Pomba Gira, second in prominence after Maria Padilha among the pantheon's female spirits of the streets. She is worked with playing cards, gold jewelry, and perfume for love, divination, and the opening of roads, drawing on the Iberian-Romani folk image of the fortune-telling gypsy woman spiritized into a Quimbanda entity.
figures.facet: She shows Pomba Gira as a title held by a court of related but distinct spirits rather than a single figure — practice recognizes dozens of named Pomba Giras, each keyed to a different folk archetype of transgressive femininity, of which the Gypsy and Maria Padilha are simply the best attested.
figures.provenance: Kelly E. Hayes, Holy Harlots: Femininity, Sexuality, and Black Magic in Brazil (2011); Nicholaj de Mattos Frisvold, Pomba Gira and the Quimbanda of Mbumba Nzila (2011).

figures.gloss: The delivery of an offering — farofa (manioc flour), cachaça, cigars, candles, and red-and-black cloth — left at a crossroads (encruzilhada), cemetery gate, or other liminal point, as payment to the Exus and Pomba Giras for a working and the vehicle that dispatches the spirits' action into the world.
figures.facet: It is the tradition's most publicly visible act of religion — unlike the indoor rites of seating a spirit (assentamento) or drawing its sigil (ponto riscado), the despacho is deliberately placed where the wider, often hostile, non-practitioner public will see and avoid it, itself a form of boundary-marking.
figures.provenance: Danilo Coppini, Quimbanda: The Cult of the Red and Black Flame (2019); Stefania Capone, Searching for Africa in Brazil (2010).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: Exu Rei is the sovereign intelligence that governs and delegates power across all Seven Kingdoms.
Intra-tradition: The drawn seal summons the spirit, while the seated vessel gives it a permanent physical body to inhabit.
Intra-tradition: Maioral is the supreme esoteric principle, while Exu Rei is the active monarch who rules the working spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Derives its core trickster and crossroads identity from the Yoruba Eshu, but alchemized into a supreme agent of left-hand sorcery.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A direct continuation of Kongo vessel magic, where spirits are physically housed in empowered containers of earth and blood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmology of Quimbanda's realms heavily adapts the Kongo cosmogram's division of the physical and spiritual worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A skeletal lord of the underworld who consumes the dead and rules over the stark reality of the grave.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fearsome, heavily armed guardian of the underworld boundary who marshals the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fiercely independent queen of desire and underworld power, weaponizing sexuality to subordinate male authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A hot-tempered feminine force tied to marginalized communities, offering explosive power, vengeance, and immediate results.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The patron of boundaries, travelers, and thieves; a cunning rogue who navigates the streets and outwits the rigid laws of society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A cultural rule-breaker and trickster who uses charm and intellect to circumvent brute force and societal norms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An ultimate principle of opposition and rebellion, often demonized by orthodox systems but essential to the cosmic tension of the left-hand path.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme deity transcending dualistic morality, containing both light and absolute darkness in one terrifying whole.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shares deep historical roots with the Fon master of languages and doorways, recontextualized as a monarch of sorcery.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A sovereign intimately tied to the literal graveyard and the physical reality of decay and ancestral bones.
Intra-tradition: one of the working chiefs of the crossroads lines serving under the King's Seven Kingdoms hierarchy.
Intra-tradition: the child-Exu class is classified and worked under its own line within the Seven Kingdoms structure.
Intra-tradition: both hold the Pomba Gira title within the same court/hierarchy of female street spirits.

the Pomba Gira figure-type is shared across the Umbanda/Quimbanda boundary, with devotees, iconography, and named entities crossing between the two closely related Afro-Brazilian traditions in practice.
Intra-tradition: the assentamento houses and feeds the spirit at home; the despacho pays and dispatches it in the field.
Intra-tradition: the sigil summons the spirit to the working; the despacho concludes and discharges it.
The identical chalk-sigil practice, shared by name and function across Umbanda and its left-hand offshoot Quimbanda.

Both are Kongo-derived ground-drawn sigils summoning a specific spirit to a specific place — Haitian vèvè and Brazilian ponto riscado are parallel New World developments of the same Kongo cosmogram (dikenga) drawing tradition.

Same Afro-Brazilian spirit-class across sibling religions: Umbanda works Pomba Gira under the law of charity, bound to evolve; Quimbanda enthrones Maria Padilha as a sovereign sorceress answering to no evolutionary law.

dossier.essence: Quimbanda is an Afro-Brazilian magico-religious tradition that crystallized in early-to-mid 20th-century urban Brazil (especially Rio de Janeiro), working directly with the spirits Exu and Pomba Gira for concrete, this-worldly ends — love, money, protection, and revenge. It fuses Kongo-Angolan ('Bantu') ritual technology, Yoruba-derived Exu figures, popular Catholicism, and Kardecist Spiritism, and grew up entangled with — and often defined against — Umbanda. It is initiatory and practitioner-centered rather than congregational, and is frequently self-described as a 'left-hand' path of sorcery.
dossier.cosmos: Quimbanda is oriented to operative work rather than to a systematic cosmogony or doctrine of the afterlife; its cosmology is a working geography of spirits. The spirit-world is divided into the 'kingdoms' (reinos) of Exu — crossroads, cemetery, forest, souls, and others — each with legions governing a domain of the urban and natural landscape, beneath a supreme figure (Exu Maioral, esoterically identified with Lucifer) and a sovereign Exu-king. The Exus and Pomba Giras are widely understood as spirits of the transgressive dead — rogues, streetwalkers, gamblers — now working magic for the living, though some lineages treat them as primordial intelligences instead.
dossier.practice: Work centers on the assentamento, a consecrated iron or clay vessel that 'seats' a spirit in the ritual space; the ponto riscado, a chalk sigil that summons a specific Exu; and sung invocations (pontos cantados), with offerings of cachaça and tobacco and animal sacrifice at crossroads and cemeteries. Initiated mediums incorporate the Exus and Pomba Giras through spirit-possession in the terreiro (house). The ethic is frankly pragmatic and transactional — pact, tribute, and feeding rather than moral purification.
dossier.canon: No scripture — an oral, initiatory tradition transmitted within houses, now supplemented by a substantial modern practitioner literature and documented ethnographically.
dossier.reflects: Religion organized frankly around pragmatic, morally unbounded power — the sacred approached as a company of spirits to be paid and put to work for concrete results in this world.
sources: Kelly E. Hayes, 'Holy Harlots: Femininity, Sexuality, and Black Magic in Brazil' (2011)
Stefania Capone, 'Searching for Africa in Brazil: Power and Tradition in Candomblé' (2010)
Diana DeG. Brown, 'Umbanda: Religion and Politics in Urban Brazil' (1986)
